title: Greg Sankey addresses discussions about possible SEC breakaway: ‘They’re real’
description: After Yahoo! Sports reported last week the SEC was considering “Plan B” amid talks about the Protect College Sports Act, commissioner Greg Sankey confirmed those talks are not just a negotiating play. He said the discussions are “real” as the college athletics landscape shifts. “You phrased the question, ‘How real?’ So I’ll take that as, […]
fullText:
After Yahoo! Sports reported last week the SEC was considering “Plan B” amid talks about the Protect College Sports Act, commissioner Greg Sankey confirmed those talks are not just a negotiating play. He said the discussions are “real” as the college athletics landscape shifts.

“You phrased the question, ‘How real?’ So I’ll take that as, like, real or not. So they’re real,” Sankey said at SEC media days. “People have talked about that. They’ve opined about the frustrations that bring them to the point of saying we should look at something significantly different.

“I do not believe that is a leverage point. I think that’s just honest communication. I would prefer that stay in a meeting room or on a video conference and not be part of the D1.ticker the next morning, but I don’t control all of that.”

title: SEC football media days will return to Nashville in 2027
description: SEC commissioner Greg Sankey announced that Nashville will host next year's SEC football media days on July 12-15, 2027.
fullText:
SEC football media days will return to Nashville.

SEC commissioner Greg Sankey announced that Nashville will host the league's annual preseason event on July 12-15, 2027.

He revealed the decision in his opening remarks of this year's media days in Tampa, Florida on July 20, kicking off the four-day event will last through July 23 at the Tampa Marriott Water Street and JW Marriott. Coaches and players representing each team will appear on SEC Network throughout.

The event was held in Nashville once before in 2023. Before it traveled to Atlanta in 2018, the event had never before been held outside of the Birmingham, Alabama, metro area. Since then, it has been held in Atlanta twice more (2022 and 2025), Nashville, Dallas (2024) and now Tampa.

Emmett Siegel covers Tennessee baseball for Knox News. Email: emmett.siegel@knoxnews.com; X: @EmmettSiegel_

This article originally appeared on Nashville Tennessean: SEC football media days will return to Nashville in 2027

title: Modder Makes GTA: Vice City Playable Inside GTA 3 Inside GTA: San Andreas
description: A modder made the entirety of Grand Theft Auto playable as a texture within another Grand Theft Auto and took it to the logical conclusion
fullText:
Ever find yourself playing 2004’s Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas thinking, “I wish I could be playing a different Grand Theft Auto right now. At the same time. Golly, I wish I could be playing three Grand Theft Autos simultaneously.” Well, fantastic news, you. A modder figured out how to get the previous installations working as a texture within the game, turning Grand Theft Auto III into Grand Theft Auto³.

Earlier this week, Grand Theft Auto modder Dryxio posted a video playing Grand Theft Auto III on a TV within Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas. In the first few seconds it appears as a gag, but soon it becomes clear that the modder is capable of running the entire game, from start to finish.

The same video demonstrates that Dryxio could also boot up Vice City on the same set. Within 48 hours, the modder upped the ante, giving CJ a gigantic, cul de sac-wide flat screen so he could play Grand Theft Auto: Vice City on a billboard within Grand Theft Auto III. Each game rendered into a real time texture, lapsing over each other. Capable of mission progression, save files, the works. Condensing the legendary trio of Rockstar sandbox games into a singular lucid, Inception-style thought bubble.

We are still in the calm before the storm before the all-consuming Grand Theft Auto VI lands in November, a landfall so heavy its gravity is bending all the other games around it. This is all enabled by the post-2000s legacy of the series, where Rockstar’s smooth-running, mayhem generating trilogy expanded what was possible on consoles and upended the industry. Liberty City, Vice City and San Andreas seemed like dense, living metropolises in their day. That Rockstar could smush them on to a PSP disc seemed like total sorcery.

Now the expanse and fidelity expected from players is in the stratosphere, and getting a new Grand Theft Auto to that level has been a tortured affair. Meanwhile, all three games that set these standards can be run on an in-game TV that traditionally runs My Five Uncles.

title: Define less, check more: Pyrefly now supports attrs
description: attrs is the library that taught Python to write classes without boilerplate. Pyrefly now supports attrs out of the box, recognizing both its classic and modern APIs with no plugins or configuration.
fullText:
Built-in support for attrs is available in Pyrefly as
of version 1.2.0-dev.1, and ships in the upcoming 1.2.0 stable release. You get
accurate constructor signatures and better type safety for your attrs classes,
with no plugins or configuration to set up.

attrs consists of two separate APIs with quite different semantics: a classic
one under the attr namespace and a modern one under attrs. While
PEP 681's dataclass_transform provides a
useful baseline for type checkers to understand parts of attrs, many features
cannot be expressed in terms of standard type annotations.

Historically, attrs users that want type checking have either had to use Mypy
(which implements dedicated attrs support via a plugin), limit themselves to a
subset of the API compatible with dataclass_transform, or just live with
limited type checking support.

At the time of writing, Pyrefly and Mypy are the only type checkers that provide
full support for attrs.

attrs lets you define a class by declaring its attributes, then generates the
boilerplate for you: __init__, __repr__, __eq__, ordering, hashing,
slots, and immutability. If you've used Python's built-in dataclasses, this
will feel familiar. attrs came first, and dataclasses was directly inspired by
it. attrs remains the more flexible of the two.

With that single decorator, attrs writes a typed __init__, a readable
__repr__, and value-based equality for you. If you pass the wrong thing, say
Tune(title="Blue Bossa", key=5), that's a bug you'd like to hear about before
you run the code.

So how does Pyrefly work with attrs? Here's what the support covers:

For the full list of supported features, check out the
documentation.

This is where attrs differs from most libraries a type checker has to support.
attrs has spent ten years accumulating two complete APIs:

Both are fully supported by attrs, and since the classic style was never
deprecated, the same codebase can end up containing both, even though mixing
them is generally discouraged. The catch is that they don't behave identically.
The clearest example is how each one decides which lines in your class body are
actually fields. The modern
decorators read your annotations. The classic @attr.s ignores bare annotations

auto_attribs=True:

Pyrefly reads how you wrote the class and adapts, the same way it reads a
Pydantic model's strict or extra settings. Because it resolves this per
class, a base in one style and a subclass in the other compose exactly as they
do at runtime.

The twist hiding in attrs is that the constructor doesn't always look like the
class. Add a converter to a field and the __init__ parameter takes the
converter's input type, while the attribute keeps its output type, and
Pyrefly tracks both:

Chart("120 bpm") type-checks because the converter accepts a str, while
medium_swing.tempo is an int everywhere you read it. That's the kind of
detail you'd otherwise only discover at runtime.

There are no special configurations or plugins required to start using Pyrefly
with attrs:

Pyrefly does not require attrs-specific configuration and recognizes both the
classic and modern APIs when available.

If you'd like to play with some examples, we put together
a demo repo you can clone
and try out.

Try attrs support in your own projects and let us know what works and what
doesn't. You can open an issue on GitHub
or connect with us on Discord.

attrs joins Pydantic and Django in the set of libraries Pyrefly supports without
a plugin. If there's a particular package you rely on, let us know.

title: A deep dive into my Forgejo setup
description: I've finally found the will to setup my own personal Git forge. In this article I'm going to present in details my set up and discuss pros and cons that come with it
fullText:
Like many others recently I got fed up by what seems to be a steady and inexorable degradation of GitHub quality of service (more on that later) and decided to walk down the self host path for all my things Git, Docker, PRs and CI/CD.

In this article I’m going to show you with some level of detail how my new self hosted Forgejo1 setup currently looks like and how well it fits me as a near total replacement for GitHub (spoilers ahead: very well).

I’ll provide links to actual repositories plus snippets of reusable configuration in case you might want to try it for yourself.

To be honest I didn’t spend much time selecting which OpenSource git forge to adopt.

I heard well about other options like SourceHut for example BUT

Not exhaustive and much for an heuristic, I suppose, but has been enough for me.

The forge is live at https://forge.l3x.in and is already home for a few of my OpenSource projects and all of my private repositories, with plans to migrate there most of the others I currently have on GitHub (more on that later).

Everything so far works pretty well: the web UI feels familiar and snappy, migrating / mirroring repos is just a click away, and CI/CD latency and build times are fantastic ⚡️ Just a quick comparison from a single project involving Docker (Podman) build to prove the point:

Almost 18 minutes saved for building (and pushing to registry) the very same application, not to mention from now on I won’t have to wait for my turn in the GH build queue.

This win alone might have been worth the effort, I’ll expand on what works better than on GitHub and what not later on but let’s get into some technical details first.

A comprehensive list of the components making up my forge:

I pay an annual subscription (~60€) to OVH3 in exchange for a dedicated Linux amd64 VPS with 4 vCPUs cores, 8GB of ram, 75 GB of storage, plus one fixed IPv4 and one IPv6 address. It might not sound like much but you’d be surprised by how much something so low profile can still punch nowadays 🥊

Nothing fancy on the OS side either: I’m an old time Debian user and I already have a bunch of Ansible roles to setup on said VPS common services like SSHd, Postfix and so on; it comes natural to just drop another role for a new service whenever a new itch needs scratching.

What changed now thanks to having my personal Forgejo is how I run config management / IaC tools like Ansible and OpenTofu. More on that later.

For this project I decided to teach myself some Podman, with me having been using Docker for all of my containerize history so far.

TL;DR: Docker Desktop is gone from my MacBook as I now use Podman exclusively for all the things containers both on my macOS workstation and Linux servers.

Contrary as with Docker, with Podman there’s no need for any dedicated daemon process proxying commands and Quadlets work remarkably well in tandem with Systemd to run containerized services.

I now run all Podman containers as rootless and once the service unit files are created I can for example start/stop/inspect services with the usual systemctl / journalctl commands you might be already familiar with.

Once services are enabled (systemctl enable), Systemd will take care of keeping them up and running after a host reboot, application crash, accidental podman kill, etc.

This is how the unit file for Forgejo looks like:

The Pull=newer is there to ensure I’m always running an up-to-date Forgejo stable version (a daily timer backup script takes care of restarting the service, that’s when the image pull kicks in).

This is how I can inspect service’ status:

You might have noticed the --user flag for all the commands: that’s required to interact with Systemd services not executed by root.

Actually I was lying not very accurate when listing the tools in use…

The stack isn’t complete without at least4 mentioning all the packages required by Podman to run properly, networking and all the rest.

This is the actual list of packages, straight from my podman Ansible role:

I won’t go into details on these to keep this article scope manageble, that said they are all OSS packages shipped with a modern Linux distribution so finding documentation is trivial, perhaps though referring directly to Podman tutorials is all you need to get everything up and running as you see fit.

I use Systemd features as much as possible including custom timers, a modern take on cronjob.

For example I run both Restic backups (see later section) and Podman prune tasks regularly, usually at daily frequency but that varies depending on the task.

An important one when running CI/CD jobs with Podman is to prune the various byproducts to avoid bloating the disk with unused waste; this timer does the trick:

in tandem with the corresponding service:

Tip: systemctl --user list-timers to show all the timers defined for the logged in user:

Boring! It was such a smooth transition away from Nginx and Certbot. Do yourself a favour and consider it too, I bet you won’t regret it.

It comes handy to be able to interact with the hosting operating system from within the CI/CD environment, in my case from Forgejo runners jobs.

For this purpose I’m using https://github.com/adnanh/webhook and it’s currently configured something like this:

The above exposes a UNIX socket reachable from the Podman containers that accepts requests from the CI/CD jobs, in this particular case it restarts another Podman container (gist), you can see the actual workflow here which uses this reusable workflow, all boiling down to a simple curl request:

The actual webhook process is not running as root so sudo is used in the webhook command to allow the webhook process to restart services. Sudo configuration at /etc/sudoers.d/webhook looks like this:

with podctl being a simple shell wrapper to run commands for the podman user (to avoid having to add --user to systemd commands and so on).

As already mentioned I use Ansible to configure the totality of services running on my personal Linux hosts.

I don’t love the tool but I still have to find a decent replacement5; once you have it in place though it’s trivial to add a new role to install this and that and assign it to host1 but not host2, and so on.

The forgejo main.yaml Ansible task list looks like this (comments and extra validation steps redacted to keep it short):

My playbooks are supposed to be idempotent so a change to the ansible repository merged into master branch triggers a job that enforce the wanted state to all my hosts, more on that in the GitOps section below.

For this specific project OpenTofu doesn’t pull much weight but it plays its part so I’ll briefly mention it too.

I keep Tofu state files for various projects in an AWS bucket and the AWS IAM role that’s dedicated to edit the bucket is assumed dynamically thanks to the Forgejo’s OAuth2 provider.

One private repository with associated tofu apply job manages a few related resources like S3 backup buckets, Axiom datasets, and so on. Same as for Ansible, more details in the GitOps section below.

The Forgejo action runner is deployed via Ansible as Podman rootless service like Forgejo, the nice extra is that it auto registers to the Forgejo instance at install time:

This works only when forgejo_runner_external: false, external runners still need manual registration, it should also be possible to fully orchestrate registration for these but it’s overkill for my use case so I didn’t bother.

You can use whatever Docker images you like as base for the runner jobs and select them within workflows with runs-on: <runner's label> , now that I have a forge and a registry I can maintain my own default image with ease: https://forge.l3x.in/alex/runner-image.

This brings a few nice benefits including container boot time (the same Docker socket is used for running the CI/CD job AND building/pushing/pulling the runner image) and generally more efficient jobs given that all the tools I generally use in my workflows (AWS CLI, Go/Deno/NodeJS runtimes, etc.) are already installed.

My Renovate bot repository is public, its daily workflow takes care of auto-discovering every repository (owned by my user) in the forge and open pull requests accordingly. It also keeps a Dependency Dashboard issue for each project with the current state of the bot affairs and actionable points (rebase this PR, rebase them all, etc) for each managed repository.

Renovate takes care of bumping package and image versions pinned in Ansible roles, OpenTofu config, and even container images like the one run by the Forgejo runner itself.

OVH Cloud offers S3 compatible object storage and I use that (as well as Scaleway’s) as Restic backups backend.

The config file at /etc/restic/restic.env looks something like this:

The actual backup scripts run as timers and are very simple restic wrappers responsible for stopping/starting services while extracting data from the related Podman volumes.

I use Vector to forward journald logs to an Axiom server-logs dataset, which is also provisioned via OpenTofu:

This is an area I still have to work on though: services like Axiom are incredibly powerful but the learning curve for how to make the best out of your data is arguably steep.

For now I’m simply dumping everything collected by journald there (their free tier is pretty generous) and getting alerted every hour if the number of error logs is above a certain threshold, better than nothing but lots of room for improvement.

As already mentioned above, all the config management / IaC repositories are now hosted on the forge. To apply any changes to the host and/or its services I now do the following:

That’s it! Of course I can still apply the same changes from my personal computer if needed, for example when I’m prototyping something, but I’m relying more and more on the full automated GitOps flow and I’ve got to say I quite like the self contained nature of it.

For a starter it’s easier to troubleshoot failures when logs are stored as job run’s so I can e.g. share them to a collaborator, point a coding agent to them, or simply save the run link in an issue and come back to that task later, download and read/search them locally, and so on.

Probably though what I like best is the simplicity of the workflow that comes with it: any approved code change automatically results in an actual applied change and I can prepare and make such modifications wherever I am (that is, from my mobile’s browser too).

As you might have already noticed so far, I’ve put quite some trust in Forgejo.

Only time will tell if I’m right about this but I tend to believe it’s easier to keep that safe compared to my workstation. A few thoughts on the matter:

I acknowledge the worse case scenario (e.g. angry ex girlfriend exfiltrating all my precious secrets out of my Forgejo’s user account) is bad. I could for example go 

Ultimately, at least for now, I accept the trade-off: we can’t have anything 100% secure anyway and comfort in life is important too. Security is also a process: I’ll keep working on this setup to find the best balance for my needs, following best practices as well as I can and as soon as they are consolidated.

For now I put my trust in the stack I chose and especially in Forgejo developers to do a good job, so far I have all the reasons to believe they are worthy my trust. You might want to consider adopting a stronger security stance if your scenario is different. I can’t prove it but personally I believe these secrets are now safer in Forgejo hands than GitHub’s anyway but you might want to consider adopting a stronger security stance if your scenario is different.

I list here some of what I feel like big improvements I gained so far, in random order of importance:

Unfortunately for now it is not feasible for me to move all projects out of GH and not only because of technical reasons:

I don’t even know where to start from… so I start with my final thought: I don’t hate GitHub, it’s been inspiring (albeit that feeling is long gone) and instrumental to my SWE career and personal enjoyment of learning how to build software, I never paid GH a single cent to make use of its many services and it’s still a relatively good vehicle for collaboration on projects I care about.

That said, I lost all the hope of any greatness for it since Microsoft’s acquisition, I just wish it will eventually loose the central role it has covered for OSS development so far

My hope is that federation between all code forges will be the default method of code collaboration, similarly to how I hope something like ATproto6 to be the de-facto backbone for social network interactions.

Many others have discussed better than me what’s wrong with GitHub today. I don’t even get started with how GH Actions have been neglected, but let me vent out at least about this small and almost invisible bit:

If find their lack of IPv6 support… disturbing embarrassing. It might not affect you directly but to me it speaks volumes about what the priorities are for one of the most successful and resourceful tech companies in the world7.

Working on my personal projects I make heavy use of LLM coding assistance, I also develop and maintain this Pi coding agent action and I have it enabled for all my Forgejo and GitHub projects, that’s also something I might blog about eventually.

That said, I wrote every single word of this post (and all the others before this) without any AI aid with the only exception of Obsidian’s auto correction (English is not my first language so I bet it shows in my writing…) and I’d be sad to know my articles get automatically marked as slop and therefore ignored, and that not because the (lack of) merits of their content.

If you want to know more on my thoughts and tooling around AI you can visit the /ai slash page8 on this very website.

It’s been a long while since the last blog post and finally I have found something (hopefully) interesting to show! Or better said, I found the will to put the effort into writing about it.

I find it much easier to build than to show and this is good exercise for me to share what I do and, tangentially, do it in a way that makes it possible to share.

I’m not sure yet I’ll stick to a predictable cadence but I’ll definitely try to write more frequently; I’ve experimented writing this article with Obsidian apps (macOS/iOS) and it’s been a surprisingly good experience so I have at least some good reasons to hope.

I use Restic to backup various other data sources like Postgres database dumps but that’s out of scope for this article ↩

I feel like recommending OVH as service provider if you want cheap but reliable cloud resources from an European company with datacenters in Europe (and elsewhere) ↩

I’m also glossing over other core components like e.g. DNS records and SMTP relaying, I consider them to be out of scope for this article ↩

has anyone been successful yet with “LLM: let’s rewrite Ansible in Go, do not do mistakes” kind of prompt? Let me know! ↩

I recently moved from Mastodon to Bluesky and I host my own PDS at https://social.l3x.in ↩

“just choke copilot down every possible throat” seems to be their current business strategy ↩

See https://slashpages.net/ for more about slash pages and the Indie Web ↩

title: Jeff Bezos and UK government invest in £2bn British startup CuspAI
description: Company aims to develop AI software that cuts research times and use of rare metals in chipmakers’ supply...
fullText:
Company aims to develop AI software that cuts research times and use of rare metals in chipmakers’ supply chains

Amazon’s founder, Jeff Bezos, and the UK government have invested in a £2bn British artificial intelligence startup that is aiming to become the “search engine for rare materials” that accelerates the next wave of technological breakthroughs.

The Cambridge-based CuspAI has raised $450m (£330m) in funding from investors including Bezos and the government’s sovereign AI fund, valuing the two-year-old business at $2.6bn.

The company has launched the AI Materials Foundry, a coalition of more than 48 tech companies, industrial firms and research facilities, with the aim of building software underpinned by artificial intelligence to discover and develop new materials for chipmakers and other industries.

CuspAI hopes to significantly cut research times and reduce or eliminate the use of rare metals – including iridium and ruthenium – in chipmakers’ supply chains.

The coalition, which includes the chipmaker Nvidia, the Facebook and Instagram owner, Meta Platforms, and the carmaker Hyundai, aims to use frontier AI, the most advanced, general-purpose artificial intelligence models, to discover new materials that could make energy networks and batteries more efficient and advance the power of semiconductors that underpin the AI revolution.

“If we don’t make progress fast, the next 50 years of industrial progress will be constrained by a single challenge: the world needs materials that don’t yet exist,” said the CuspAI co-founders Dr Chad Edwards and Prof Max Welling. “That’s what we’re on a mission to solve – combining frontier agentic AI with deep domain expertise, exclusive data access and close customer partnerships. The world simply can’t make the progress it needs to with known materials, that much is clear.”

The company said the round of series B funding would enable it to expand internationally, opening a new office in Singapore and increasing staff numbers in Cambridge, Amsterdam, Berlin, Tokyo and the US.

Josh Coyne, a partner at Kleiner Perkins, the venture capital firm that co-led the latest round of funding, said: “Most big leaps in technology come down to a material, and the next set, from cheaper carbon capture to semiconductors and cleaner water, is stuck waiting on materials nobody has discovered yet. CuspAI built a search engine that changes that.”

The latest fundraise followed a $100m series A investment last year.

CuspAI is the fourth company to receive funding from the government’s fund targeting investment in early-stage British AI firms to help them scale up to compete on the world stage. The government did not disclose the size of the investment.

Criteria for receiving funding from the sovereign AI fund, which makes a typical equity investment in a startup of between £1m and £10m, include having a main office in the UK and the company’s founders being British.

Liz Kendall, the science and technology secretary, said: “CuspAI is putting the power of AI to work to help us tackle some of the biggest challenges facing the UK and the world today, such as energy and climate change. By backing British companies such as this we are paving the way for breakthroughs that are set to grow our economy, protect the natural world and improve everyone’s lives.”

CuspAI has brought on board John Giannandrea, a former Apple and Google executive, who is helping set up its US operations.

The company is also working with Abhi Talwalkar, a board member at chip maker AMD and chair of Lam Research.

title: Experts warn of rise in sadistic online exploitation of vulnerable children
description:
Global increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse material
Cases of predators coercing children into producing sexual photos and videos of themselves have surged globally in the past year, a cruel phenomenon known as “sadistic online exploitation”, child safety experts and law enforcement officials warn.
In cases of sadistic online exploitation, perpetrators target vulnerable teens on social media platforms and online games. These predators then compel their victims into producing child sexual abuse material (CSAM), harming themselves and others, or, in extreme cases, attempting suicide.
In the US, call or text the Childhelp abuse hotline on 800-422-4453 or visit their website for more resources and to report child abuse or DM for help. For adult survivors of child abuse, help is available at ascasupport.
fullText:
Global increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse material

Cases of predators coercing children into producing sexual photos and videos of themselves have surged globally in the past year, a cruel phenomenon known as “sadistic online exploitation”, child safety experts and law enforcement officials warn.

In cases of sadistic online exploitation, perpetrators target vulnerable teens on social media platforms and online games. These predators then compel their victims into producing child sexual abuse material (CSAM), harming themselves and others, or, in extreme cases, attempting suicide.

“We often see that the offenders will communicate with that child and, through that initial communication, ask them for nude or explicit imagery of themselves,” said Kathryn Rifenbark, a director at the National Center of Missing and Exploited Children (NCMEC). “Then they’re using that as a leverage to cause the child to harm themselves or harm other children or other people in their home or create more explicit imagery of them or other children in their home.”

Recent cases include the June sentencing of a 21-year-old Maryland man to 30 years in prison for sexually exploiting 10 female minors and cyberstalking.

The perpetrator “used the internet to create and share extreme content – such as gore, violence, and child sexual abuse material – to extort and blackmail vulnerable teenagers”, and the man sexually exploited his victims and encouraged them to engage in self-harm, according to a Department of Justice press release.

The NCMEC received more than 3,000 reports of sadistic online exploitation worldwide in 2025, a 125% increase from the previous year. But those cases represent only a small fraction of the total number of incidents, experts say, because the NCMEC is only notified if child sexual abuse material has been detected and reported.

Predators targeting children often belong to online networks, and are often teens or young adults themselves. To recruit their victims, the perpetrators engage with video games popular with children, such as Roblox and Minecraft. A spokesperson for Microsoft, which owns Minecraft, said: “Our goal is to make Minecraft one of the hardest places for bad actors to operate, and we have a variety of tools we use to help foster a safe online experience, such as parental controls, chat filtering, in-game reporting, and dedicated moderation teams.”

They may also search social media groups that focus on mental health issues such as eating disorders or suicide ideation because they are specifically looking for vulnerable children to target and groom, child safety experts interviewed said.

Perpetrators of sadistic online exploitation who coerce children into inflicting other forms of harm on themselves fall outside those reports.

The coerced abuses can include forcing children to cut themselves, eat their own vomit, drink urine, die by suicide, or harm other children and pets in their homes. Some groups also engage in mass casualty planning, doxing and cyber-harassment.

A common form of abuse is forcing victims to carve perpetrators’ social media usernames on their bodies, said Amanda Goharian, director of research at Thorn, a child safety non-profit.

“One of the key things in these groups is that the level of severity of the harms that are being committed are extreme, and they can rapidly develop into outcomes such as completed suicides,” said Goharian.

In February, the FBI issued a warning to parents and caregivers about the “sharp increase” in the activity of violent online networks, including the “764” group, targeting and exploiting children and other vulnerable individuals.

“The term nihilistic violent extremism (NVE) refers to what is motivating most of these subjects to commit criminal acts. They want to sow chaos and facilitate the destruction of society,” said the letter. “These subjects may be engaging in criminal activity for sexual gratification, social status, a sense of belonging, or for a mix of other reasons.”

The predators target vulnerable children who have disclosed personal information online about the struggles they face, such as mental health challenges, eating disorders, self-harm or suicidal ideation, via gaming or social media platforms. These children are looking for connections and support online, which can render them more susceptible to manipulation, said the experts interviewed.

Predators typically invite victims to move the conversation to a messaging app such as Telegram, which, according to Rifenbark, “doesn’t report very much” to the NCMEC, meaning the organization has limited visibility into potential child sexual exploitation.

It’s on these messaging platforms where the sadistic online exploitation takes place. Telegram did not respond to a request for comment.

Many of the perpetrators have troubled lives or are socially isolated in real life, and are looking for notoriety online as a form of empowerment and attention from others, experts interviewed said.

“These groups are focused on harming children and targeting children for the purposes of clout and like gaining hierarchy and power both within their groups and over the victims,” said Maria Thomas, investigator at Rise Information Security Foundation, a non-profit focused on cybersecurity. “Teens join them because it’s seen as a subversive and edgy thing to do.”

The perpetrators gain status within their groups from posting evidence of their abuse, images such as dossiers of photos and screenshots of messages with their victims, which are known as “lore books”. Typically, these collections are uploaded on to a Telegram channel labeled as “Victim Name Lore Book”, experts said.

“Harm is perpetrated as a way to essentially gain clout within the network, and to be elevated,” said Goharian.

Organized nihilistic violent extremism and sadistic online exploitation were first documented in the 2010s, when a youth cybergang called the Com, which focused on hacking and identity theft before some subsets shifted into child exploitation. Splinter networks, such as CVLT, an online group that espoused neo-Nazism, nihilism and pedophilia as its core principles, were formed. The group 764 was formed in 2021 by a teenager in Texas who had learned grooming and exploitation methods as a member of CVLT.

Online exploitation can escalate into dramatic real-world violence, experts warn. In February, an 18-year-old man from Pennsylvania was arrested and charged with grooming a 13-year-old girl he met on social gaming platform Discord, allegedly convincing her to send him sexually explicit material of herself and images of self-harm. The man travelled across the country to engage in sexual activity with the girl in a motel, according to the US justice department. “Discord has a longstanding policy on Teen and Child Safety that prohibits sexual exploitation and predatory behavior, including content or communities that facilitate grooming,” said a Discord spokesperson.

“When law enforcement arrived at the motel room, they encountered [the man] and the victim and found condoms, a knife, lubricant, razor blades, bloody tissues,” stated a Department of Justice press release about the case. “The victim told law enforcement that [the man] and she had engaged in sexual conduct and that he had used a knife to repeatedly cut her.”

In May, a 16-year-old boy from South Carolina was arrested for engaging in exploitation tied to 764 after receiving a tip from Discord regarding possible school violence.

“Offenders have a very, very high level of control over the children. They are using that connection that they’ve built with that child to really try to control that child to do some pretty harmful things to themselves,” said Rifenbark.

Rifenbark said the NCMEC had received reports from parents who discovered what was happening to their child and took away the child’s phone, only for the child to find another device in the home, such as an old device the family was no longer using, and use it to re-engage with the offenders online, probably due to the high levels of control and grooming they have been subjected to.

“A lot of times when victims are coming and reporting to us, they’re very scared, they’re scared of retaliation from offenders, they’re worried that their personal information might get leaked, because that’s something that the offenders are doing as part of their victimization,” Rifenbark said.

According to Goharian, the networks are highly persistent, with individual perpetrators often operating hundreds of accounts. If one account is reported for violating a platform’s terms of service and removed, they can quickly switch to “shadow” accounts that are already established and immediately resume their activity.

According to a state prosecutor, speaking on the condition of anonymity out of concern for professional reprisals, some juveniles who commit sexual abuse do not outgrow the behavior but often go on to offend as adults. Better interventions are needed for young offenders before their behavior escalates, they said.

“We just do a really bad job sometimes of recognizing dangerous kids,” said the prosecutor. “I don’t think these kids are going to grow out of it. I think they’re growing into it.”

In the US, call or text the Childhelp abuse hotline on 800-422-4453 or visit their website for more resources and to report child abuse or DM for help. For adult survivors of child abuse, help is available at ascasupport.org. In the UK, the NSPCC offers support to children on 0800 1111, and adults concerned about a child on 0808 800 5000. The National Association for People Abused in Childhood (Napac) offers support for adult survivors on 0808 801 0331. In Australia, children, young adults, parents and teachers can contact the Kids Helpline on 1800 55 1800, or Bravehearts on 1800 272 831, and adult survivors can contact Blue Knot Foundation on 1300 657 380. Other sources of help can be found at Child Helplines International

title: iOS 27 Comes With a macOS-Like Recovery Mode for iPhone
description: You don't need to connect your iPhone to a computer to troubleshoot issues.
fullText:
Your iPhone is a mini computer, for all intents and purposes, and yet, some essential iOS features still require a Mac or a PC. That includes system recovery: If your iPhone is facing software issues, you've traditionally needed a computer to restore it. Apple is changing that slowly: In iOS 26, the company rolled out a new Recovery Assistant, which lets you triage your iPhone directly within iOS, as if it were a Mac. However, that menu would only appear if your iPhone failed on its own, with no way for you to access it yourself. That changes with iOS 27. If you're running the beta, you can try out Recovery Assistant whenever you want, with a new menu to boot.

If your iPhone is glitching out, freezing, or has other software problems, you can use iOS 27's recovery mode to identify and fix the problem. Apple Support can also use recovery mode to run diagnostics on your iPhone without connecting it to a computer. The new recovery mode has the following five options:

Recovery Assistant: With this, iOS automatically identifies and tries to fix software issues on your iPhone.

Software Update: This option uses a nearby iPhone or iPad to update your iOS device to the latest version. This could help if your device is having trouble booting into an older version of the operating system. Note that this feature isn't available on older devices. (I can't access it on my iPhone 14 Pro Max.)

Diagnostics Mode: This option helps Apple employees diagnose problems and decide how to proceed with repair. It's also how Apple checks your iPhone for defects during the trade-in process. If you're planning to sell your iPhone, you may use this feature to identify issues with your device.

Erase All Content and Settings: This option lets you format your iPhone and reset it to factory settings. Again, this option is helpful if your iPhone is unable to boot into iOS.

Recovery Mode: This option lets you connect your iPhone to a computer and restore it to factory settings.

It's quite easy to access recovery mode in iOS 27, but you should only do so as a last resort. Multiple options in recovery mode actively point you towards formatting your iPhone and losing all data. For most software-related issues, you should only use Recovery Assistant if other common troubleshooting methods fail. Before you start using recovery modes regularly, try restarting your device once, updating to the latest version of iOS, or reinstalling any problematic apps. Once you've tried the above steps, you can fire up recovery mode on your iPhone by doing the following:

Press Volume Up and release it.

Press Volume Down and release it.

Immediately after this, hold down the Side button (power button).

Keep holding the button even after the Apple logo appears. You'll see a message that reads, "Continue holding for recovery."

You can release the power button when you see "Loading recovery options…" on screen. A progress bar will appear under the Apple logo, and when it completes loading, your iPhone will load the recovery mode screen.

To exit recovery mode, hit the power button in the top-right corner of the screen, and tap Restart. This will reboot your iPhone and bring you back to its lock screen.

title: 10 Privacy Hacks for Managing Browser Cookies Everyone Should Know
description: There's more to cookies than clicking "accept" or "reject" on a website.
fullText:
Do you "accept" or "reject" that cookie request? Browser cookies don’t just remember your login details: They can track your browsing history across websites, follow you around with specific ads, or even hijack your active browser session to log into your accounts without a password. On the other hand, if you don’t click accept when you see a cookie banner, that can affect your browsing experience—many websites need cookies to function normally.

Luckily, cookie management isn’t just a binary choice any more. You can choose which cookies to accept on a website, auto-delete cookies after a set amount of time, or even switch between browser profiles to control which cookies stay active during a session.

When an attacker steals your session cookies, they can bypass the requirement for passwords or multi-factor authentication and get into your logged-in accounts right away. Websites think it’s you accessing an account you already logged into, so they don’t ask for credentials again.

DBSC (aka Device Bound Session Credentials) is a Chrome feature that restricts your session cookies so that they apply to your current physical device only. A hardware boundary is established, preventing hackers from replaying your session cookies from a different device. DBSC is turned on by default in Chrome, but you need to be using version 146 or above and enable TPM on Windows for it to work.

Blocking all third-party cookies outright often prevents any embedded content from loading, but if you don’t do it, cookies from different websites can stitch your complete browsing activity together for ad targeting.

Total Cookie Protection is the default Firefox feature that creates a separate cookie jar for each domain, so third-party cookies stay enabled but can’t track you after you leave that website. This is great, except when you’re trying to stay logged into multiple accounts on the same domain, like several inboxes on Gmail. Firefox won’t create separate cookie jars for different accounts on the same domain, so third-party cookies can follow you from one account to another.

That’s why you should install Multi-Account Containers. This official Firefox extension lets you separate cookies not just by domain name, but by creating different container tabs that each represent a distinct browsing activity like Personal, Work, or Shopping. Each container gets its own cookie storage that does not interfere with the others. This can work together with Total Cookie Protection for even more privacy benefits.

ITP, ATFP, and AFP are three overlapping cookie management systems that work together to protect your browsing activity from trackers and cookies on Safari. You should know which does what so you can enable the functions that work best for you.

Intelligent Tracking Prevention (ITP) is the default anti-tracking engine built into Safari since 2017. It causes script-set cookies to expire after seven days and purges all site-specific data after 30 days of no activity.

Advanced Tracking and Fingerprinting Protection (ATFP) is an optional feature that adds another layer of security protocols on top of ITP for domain blocking and link tracker removal.

Advanced Fingerprinting Protection (AFP) is a newer Safari feature that prevents websites from identifying your device using high-entropy device APIs that pull data like screen size, hardware specs, audio data, canvas rendering, etc.

You can adjust your privacy settings on Safari by navigating to Settings > Apps > Safari > Advanced on your iPhone or Mac.

Most people aren’t used to clearing their cookie storage manually, even though that’s something you should absolutely do. Luckily there are browser extensions that can automatically clear all cookies from a website the moment you close your browser tab, unless you’ve specifically whitelisted that website.

Cookie AutoDelete on Firefox is the original extension that introduced this feature. But on Chrome, Opera, Edge, and other Chromium-based browsers, you’ll need a newer extension like Cookie Guardian. Safari does not have a similar extension but it sets cookies to delete automatically after seven days by default, so you don’t have to worry about it so much.

Sometimes you want to remove specific tracking cookies without wiping your whole cookie session with a website. Cookie-Editor, an open-source extension that’s available across all major browsers, lets you do exactly that. While it is primarily intended as a developer tool for troubleshooting web apps, you can also use it to manage your privacy while surfing the internet.

That said, Cookie-Editor does not support rule-based automation and cookies must be deleted manually on every visit, so treat this more as a last-resort fix for persistent tracking cookies rather than something you’d use all the time.

Did you know that you can save a snapshot of your browser’s full set of active cookies, delete them, then restore them at any point to the last snapshot using specific extensions? Instead of manually logging back into accounts every time you clear your cookie storage, you can create a cookie profile to instantly snap back to the logged-in state after you delete your browsing activity.

Cookie Editor on Chromium-based browsers (not to be confused with the open-source Cookie-Editor), lets you create cookie snapshots for each site and restore them with a single click. On Firefox, you can get the same functionality out of Cookie Quick Manager. Safari does not have any extensions that support cookie profiles just yet, but you can export and import cookies in JSON, which gets you close enough.

Instead of allowing third-party cookies on every site, you can create temporary exceptions on a per-site basis for websites that need to have cookies enabled.

You can do this in Chrome by clicking on the eye-shaped icon next to the address bar, then switching “Third-party cookies blocked” to “Third-party cookies allowed.” This grants that website an exception that lasts for 90 days by default, unless you choose to disable it manually.

On Firefox, you can click the shield icon on the address bar and toggle off Enhanced Tracking Protection. Your page will now reload with trackers and cookies enabled. It won’t use tracking protection again until you manually enable it. If you visit a website occasionally and need complete functionality when you do, but don’t trust the site to have blanket cookie exceptions the year round, this could be a nice middle ground.

Passkeys protect your login credentials, but not the logged-in accounts on your web browser. If you’ve already signed into an app or website from your browser, it generates session cookies that stay active until the moment you close your browser. If an attacker gains access to those session cookies through malware or phishing, they can freely access your accounts as long as your current browser session remains active.

You can revoke your session cookies once you’re done with an app or website by simply logging out of the account from that device. But if you want to log out of all sessions from other devices too, Google, Microsoft, Meta, and all other major online platforms let you revoke active sessions tied to your account either per device or all at once.

This even works on third-party apps or websites that use the single sign-on feature to let you sign in with your Google or Microsoft account without setting a username and password.

Not all browsers support cookie management extensions on every device. If you’d like to block tracker cookies before they can even reach your browser, you can use network-level tools that block cookie requests directly from your router. Pi-hole is a self-hosted application that runs on a remote server or virtual machine. Once installed, you can configure any router to route all internet traffic through your Pi-hole server by changing some DNS settings.

This will block any known advertisement or tracking domains from sending cookie requests to your connected devices, effectively stopping tracking cookies from even being generated. If self-hosting Pi-hole feels intimidating, you can also try the cloud-based alternative NextDNS.

Once you click “Clear browsing data,” your browser permanently deletes all your cookies from the device storage and there’s no way to retrieve them. If you’d rather avoid re-authenticating logins on certain websites every time, you can back up your cookie storage before performing a cleanup by exporting it as a CSV file or JSON. You can use the Cookie-Editor extension mentioned earlier to export your cookie storage on Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari, and other popular web browsers. Other extensions like EditThisCookie and Cookies.txt offer the same feature.

title: 'Medal guarantee' Wiffen ready for Commonwealth Games
description: Daniel Wiffen tells BBC Sport NI about his breakthrough at the 2022 Commonwealth Games, dealing with his newfound fame and his unwavering self-belief.
fullText:
He has won gold in the Olympics, Worlds and Europeans since taking silver in the 2022 Commonwealth Games, but Daniel Wiffen says that second-place finish in Birmingham still "sits over my head".

Ultra-competitive by nature, Wiffen acknowledges that the Games four years ago were the springboard to the unprecedented success that has since followed, including 800m gold and 1500m bronze at the Paris Olympics in 2024.

That is why, two years out from the 2028 Olympics in Los Angeles, he is looking forward to this year's Commonwealth Games in Glasgow, where he will compete in the 400m, 800m and 1500m and aim to add more gold medals to his burgeoning collection.

"I think after Tokyo [Olympics in 2021] we had a three-year plan and the first was to medal at the Commonwealth Games. You get the first thing ticked off the list and you're on the way. It was the catalyst which started my swimming career," Wiffen told BBC Sport NI.

"The Commonwealth Games silver sits over my head as I wish I won it, but I've always said in 2026 I want to win every competition.

"I had a bit of a drought before the Europeans and I wasn't in my top form for like a year and I think Commonwealth Games, I'm looking forward to getting my redemption and getting a gold medal - at least one - and to be honest, I want three but I have to see."

Life has changed drastically for Wiffen since Birmingham four years ago as a result of his success in the pool.

He is now regularly stopped in the street for photographs and has enjoyed his newfound fame.

"I like it, it's cool people recognise the hard work you put in. I've been swimming since I was seven and my parents always said they never thought I'd become a celebrity in Ireland. You get a lot of free stuff which they like!" he joked.

Wiffen also gives an insight into life as a high-performing athlete by regularly posting YouTube videos on a channel he shares with his twin brother Nathan.

The channel has gained over 100,000 subscribers and the 24-year-old believes the experience of posting videos in which he talks to a camera has helped him with media duties as a swimmer.

"For Paris I was posting videos all the way along and people were like you should stop and hide it but I was like, to be honest, I'm training faster than anyone else and I'm making them scared," he said.

"People find media interviews daunting but I think because I started my YouTube channel when I was 18 and I've been doing that, I've become quite good on camera and quite good at being myself instead of putting on a fake persona that other people do."

Wiffen is part of Team NI's 15-strong swimming squad heading to Glasgow and is one of their genuine multiple medal contenders, although he would rather refer to himself as a "medal guarantee".

Bethany Firth, Barry McClements and Wiffen brought home NI's three medals in Birmingham, but the Magheralin man believes more medals will follow this year given the work that has gone into developing the swimming programme across the island of Ireland.

"One of the main things you notice over the years is the systems building up the athletes have got more experience from outside resources and people from different countries who have had success before and they can build the athletes up from a younger age.

"No doubt we'll have a better medal count than in Birmingham, I think we'll have multiple golds."

Wiffen's self-belief is to be admired and has only been strengthened by a tough spell in 2025 in which his form dipped as he battled back from appendicitis surgery.

He recovered to win three medals at the European short course championships, which gives him the confidence that he can bounce back from future setbacks in whatever form they take.

"Before European short course last year I had a bit of doubt and that's the first time in my life, it was because I had appendicitis and I had a month to train, from zero to 100 in a month to win the Europeans and after I did that I believe I can win anything.

"After that setback nothing is going to stop me."

This article contains content provided by Google YouTube. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. You may want to read Google’s cookie policy, external and privacy policy, external before accepting. To view this content choose ‘accept and continue’.

title: Salford re-sign half-back Sneyd from 2027
description: Salford re-sign Warrington Wolves half-back Marc Sneyd on a deal to begin from next season.
fullText:
Salford have re-signed Warrington Wolves half-back Marc Sneyd on a deal to begin from next season.

The 35-year-old joins for his third spell with the club having started his senior career with them in 2010 before returning in 2022.

Sneyd joined the Wire in March 2025 on an 18-month deal when the formerly named Red Devils were enduring a financially troubled season which culminated in their relegation from Super League and subsequent takeover and rebranding late last year.

"Timing was a big one for me. I spoke to Ryan [Brierley - CEO] and the coaches and their ambition really stood out," he said.

"The club is at a brand new stage, but I want to help the club get back to the level the club wants to be at. Hopefully next year we can build on what the club has already built this season."

Having come through the club's academy, Sneyd has made a total of 136 Salford appearances across his two spells.

"I was here when the club went through administration, and I know first hand how difficult it was for Sneydy to leave," Brierley added.

"To now have the opportunity to bring him home, and give him the chance to finish this chapter in a Salford shirt, feels incredibly special."

title: 'I turned on Ceefax, saw we'd drawn Red Star Belgrade, and almost fainted'
description: As Irish Premiership champions Larne prepare to face Red Star Belgrade in Champions League qualifying on Tuesday, ex-Portadown defender Alfie Stewart reflects on facing the then European Cup holders 35 years ago.
fullText:
When Larne step out at Inver Park to face Red Star Belgrade in the first leg of their Champions League second qualifying round tie on Tuesday, they will follow in the footsteps of another Irish League club who took on the decorated European giants in their pomp 35 years ago.

Red Star began their defence of the European Cup they won the previous season by defeating then Irish League champions Portadown 8-0 on aggregate back in the autumn of 1991.

The first leg, played in Szeged in Hungary rather than in Belgrade because of the deteriorating security situation in then Yugoslavia, saw Portadown lose 4-0.

The second leg at Shamrock Park ended in the same scoreline.

The European Cup preceded the Champions League as the continent's premier club competition and featured only the champions from the competing countries in an open draw.

The Portadown team which celebrated back-to-back domestic league titles in 1990 and 1991 included long-serving goalkeeper Mickey Keenan, defender Philip Major, midfielders Martin Russell and Paul Doolin, plus renowned Scottish strike duo Stevie Cowan and Sandy Fraser.

The central defensive partnership of Alfie Stewart and Brian Strain also played a key part in the success enjoyed by the Mid Ulster side, who were managed by legendary Irish League boss Ronnie McFall.

Speaking to BBC Sport NI's Joel Bell, Stewart recalled discovering his club's opponents courtesy of a then popular television text service.

"Back in the day the only way of finding out who you were playing in the draw was to switch on to Ceefax and that was how I found out," he said.

"I almost fainted when I found out it was Red Star Belgrade, the European champions, but you quickly switch into excited mode as it's a chance in a million.

"To go to Yugoslavia as it was then before the break-up of the Balkan States, was something to look forward to."

Red Star Belgrade tie 'free hit' for Larne - Haveron

Stewart had also played in Europe with former club Glentoran, before enjoying further "special" adventures with Portadown.

"Even though we were playing across the border, the Serbians had come across in great numbers and they were very loud and very hostile," he explained of the away leg in Hungary.

"It made the occasion a bit more difficult but it's always great walking out into an atmosphere like that as a footballer. Those are the nights that you dream of.

"When you stand beside these guys and they're 6'3'' or 6'4'', you were facing supreme athletes with sheer power and pace and it was a challenge.

"People have asked me over the years, what's it like when you play against those elite full-time players and it's simply this - they think quicker, they run quicker, they're stronger.

"You were apprehensive, but not afraid, because my attitude was to embrace it. You're in awe of them but you have a job to do and you don't want to let yourself down."

The Ports defender felt a four-goal reverse against the best team in Europe represented a creditable outcome.

"We came out at the end of the night having lost 4-0 after conceding two late goals but we were extremely proud of how we performed on the night and met the challenge.

"Sinisa Mihajlovic went on from Red Star to play most of his career in Italy with Lazio and Sampdoria. He was something special and he scored two late free-kicks from outside the box.

"When you saw those free-kicks being taken from 30 yards out you probably thought that you were safe but this guy, he ended his career being recognised as one of the greatest dead ball specialists that the game had ever seen. He stood out."

The second leg at Shamrock Park was a memorable occasion, with 1991 European Golden Boot winner Darko Pancev among the goalscorers for the illustrious visitors.

"There was great excitement. It was a tremendous night for the fans, a tremendous reward for their support," reflected Stewart.

"To have the European champions playing at Shamrock Park it is something that will never happen again, probably at any Irish League ground.

"Darko Pancev, who moved to AC Milan a year after, he was scoring goals for fun in the Yugoslav league and moved to Italy, which at that time was the place to be just as the Premier League is now. He was a fantastic striker."

Current Irish League champions Larne will host Red Star in the first leg of their tie on Tuesday, 21 July (20:00 BST), with the second leg away from home on Wednesday, 29 July (19:00 BST).

title: What has changed in The Hundred for 2026?
description: BBC Sport's Ask Me Anything team looks at what has changed for this year's edition of The Hundred.
fullText:
The 2026 edition of The Hundred gets under way on Tuesday and will have some significant changes from previous years.

In 2025, the ECB finalised the sale of stakes in all eight teams with over £500m of the funds generated set to be reinvested back into English and Welsh cricket.

As part of the changes, some team names and badges have also been updated to reflect their new owners.

Here is a look at all of the changes coming on and off the field for this year's competition.

Since the 2025 Hundred campaign ended last August, all eight teams have been either partially or fully taken over by new owners as part of significant financial investment into the competition.

Of those eight, four have gone to ownership groups that are also involved in the Indian Premier League (IPL) franchises.

Chelsea co-owner Todd Boehly is also among the new investors after purchasing a 49% stake in the Trent Rockets.

Following the influx of new ownership, there have also been some changes made to a few team news, badges and kits.

Three team names have changed to be in line with IPL clubs they are partners with. The Northern Superchargers have become Sunrisers Leeds, the Oval Invincibles are now known as MI London and the Manchester Originals have become the Manchester Super Giants.

That trio of teams have also updated their badges and kits following their re-branding.

While the Southern Brave have not changed their name, their colour scheme has switched from green and black to blue and red.

MI London have also switched colour schemes to blue and gold, in line with their partner IPL side the Mumbai Indians.

London Spirit have changed jerseys from dark blue to purple and black with a red and yellow trim.

Sunrisers Leeds have swapped from purple and white strips to orange and black.

Manchester Super Giants are yet to release their shirt for the campaign, but have changed their badge to a red, white and yellow colour scheme with an elephant on it.

Trent Rockets, Welsh Fire and Birmingham Phoenix are the only teams to not make significant changes to their badges or shirt colour schemes.

Grace Ballinger column: How will a revamped Hundred play out?

The opening match of the 2026 edition of The Hundred is in the women's competition between MI London and Sunrisers Leeds at The Oval.

That starts at 14:45 BST on Tuesday, 21 July. The two teams will meet later that evening in the men's competition.

There are daily matches from then until Wednesday, 12 August, which is the final day of the group stage in both competitions.

On Friday, 14 August the eliminator will take place in both competitions. There, the teams finishing second and third in the group stage will meet in a play-off, with the winner facing the group stage winners in the final.

The finals for both competitions take place two days later on Sunday, 16 August in a double header at Lord's.

New investment brought major changes in the player auction, which took place in March.

In both competitions, teams were allowed to pre- England white ball captain Harry Brook was the most expensive signing, staying at Sunrisers Leeds for £460,000.

England white ball batter Phil Salt signed for Welsh Fire in a £450,000 move, while England fast bowler Jofra Archer remains at Southern Brave, having been picked up for £400,000.

Archer is joined by England's Test wicketkeeper Jamie Smith, joining the Brave for £300,000.

English all-rounder James Coles broke the record for most expensive Hundred signing from the auction when joining London Spirit for £390,000. England wicket-keeper Jordan Cox also signed for Welsh Fire for £300,000.

Former England Test captain Joe Root joined Welsh Fire for £240,000, while England white-ball spinner Adil Rashid was acquired for £250,000 by Southern Brave.

England pace bowlers Josh Tongue and Gus Atkinson both signed for the Manchester Super Giants for £200,000 and £70,000 respectively.

In the women's competition, England captain Nat Sciver-Brunt and Lauren Bell were the joint-most expensive retention signings at £140,000 each. All-rounder Sciver-Brunt is staying with the Trent Rockets, with Lauren Bell staying at Southern Brave.

England all-rounder Alice Capsey has joined Birmingham Phoenix, having been sold for £130,000 in the retentions round.

Following a one-year break from the competition, India batter Smriti Mandhana has joined Manchester Super Giants for £90,000. England spinner Sophie Ecclestone is alongside, being retained at £110,000.

New Zealand all-rounder Sophie Devine and Australia wicket-keeper Beth Mooney both recorded the most expensive women's signings. The duo, both sold for £210,000, joined the Welsh Fire and Trent Rockets respectively.

England fast bowler Issy Wong has signed for Southern Brave for £130,000. Wong joins England spinners Tilly Corteen-Coleman and Sarah Glenn, who the Brave signed for £105,000 and £75,000 respectively.

Batter Tammy Beaumont, who recently called time on her international career, was signed by Birmingham Phoenix for £70,000.

2025 - MI London (formerly Oval Invincibles)

2024 - MI London (formerly Oval Invincibles)

2023 - MI London (formerly Oval Invincibles)

2025 – Sunrisers Leeds (formerly Northern Superchargers)

2022 – MI London (formerly Oval Invincibles)

2021 – MI London (formerly Oval Invincibles)

You will be able to follow the tournament across the BBC, including 16 matches live on BBC Two and BBC iPlayer.

Isa Guha, Alex Hartley and Amber Sandhu will present the coverage with Michael Vaughan, Sir Alastair Cook and Phil Tufnell returning as pundits.

New faces include England Test opener Emilio Gay and seamer Mark Wood, while former India batter Ajinkya Rahane and New Zealand wicketkeeper Katey Martin will also be involved.

World Cup-winners Tymal Mills, Ebony Rainford-Brent, Dawid Malan, Amanda Jade-Wellington and Carlos Brathwaite will also be part of the coverage.

There will be ball-by-ball audio commentary on all 68 matches on BBC Sounds, with extensive coverage on BBC Radio 5 Live and 5 Sports Extra.

The BBC Sport website and app will have reports on every game and daily video highlights of the best moments, while there will be live text commentary and in-play video clips on the Eliminator and finals.

On social media, the BBC Cricket Instagram account will also have all the best moments, plus you can keep up to date with the competition via the BBC Cricket WhatsApp channel.

BBC Sport's YouTube channel will have highlights throughout the tournament, and keep an eye out for 'Ravi meets' - a new feature with a partially-sighted cricket fan who took BBC Sport's social channels by storm in the summer of 2025.

Times are BST and subject to change (women's game first, then men's)

Tuesday, 21 July: MI London v Sunrisers Leeds (14:45 & 18:30)

Wednesday, 22 July: Southern Brave v Welsh Fire (14:45 & 18:30)

Sunday, 26 July: Trent Rockets v London Spirit (14:30 & 18:00)

Sunday, 2 August: MI London v Manchester Super Giants (14:30 & 18:00)

Tuesday, 4 August: Sunrisers Leeds v London Spirit (15:00 & 18:30)

Wednesday, 5 August: Trent Rockets v Birmingham Phoenix (15:00 & 18:30)

Sunday, 9 August: London Spirit v Birmingham Phoenix (14:30 & 18:00)

Sunday, 16 August: Finals (14:15 & 18:00)
